children.title: Quick-Start: Optics, Refraction & Retinoscopy
Core Optics Concepts
Pinhole imaging: small aperture → sharp image regardless of lens power; eliminates blur from defocus; basis of pinhole VA test (improves refractive error blur, NOT optic nerve/macular disease)
Vergence: measure of ray divergence/convergence; unit = dioptre (D) = 1/distance in metres; vergence = n/d
Vergence equation: V' = V + P; where V = incident vergence, P = lens power, V' = emergent vergence
Convex (plus) lens: converges light; real focal point; corrects hyperopia
Concave (minus) lens: diverges light; virtual focal point; corrects myopia
Combining lenses in contact: P_total = P₁ + P₂
Refraction Overview (8 Steps)
Step 1: Preliminaries — VA, old Rx, keratometry, auto-refractor estimate
Step 2: Occlude one eye
Step 3: Initial estimate — retinoscopy or auto-refractor
Step 4: Refine cylinder axis (JCC flip at axis) then cylinder power (JCC flip at 45° to axis)
Step 5: Refine sphere (fog then reduce + until best acuity; 'maximum plus for best VA')
Step 6: Repeat for other eye
Step 7: Binocular balance (prism dissociation or fogging); control accommodation
Step 8: Near add (presbyopia); assess near point, determine add power
Retinoscopy Quick-Start
With motion (WTM): reflex moves same direction as streak → add plus lenses to neutralise
Against motion (ATM): reflex moves opposite to streak → add minus lenses to neutralise
Neutralisation: reflex fills entire pupil with no motion; brightest and widest reflex
Working distance: 67cm = +1.50D, 50cm = +2.00D subtract from gross retinoscopy Rx
Fogging: keep fellow eye fogged (+5D) to control accommodation during retinoscopy
Geometric Optics
Fundamental Principles
Refractive index (n): n = c/v; speed of light in vacuum / speed in medium; higher n = slower, more bending
Snell's law: n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂; light bends toward normal entering denser medium
Critical angle: angle above which total internal reflection occurs; sinθc = n₂/n₁; basis of gonioscopy (contact lens changes critical angle)
Fermat's principle: light takes path of least optical path length (explains refraction and reflection)
Prisms
Light bends toward base; image displaced toward apex
Prism power: 1 prism dioptre (Δ) = 1cm displacement at 1m distance
Prentice's rule: prism power (Δ) = lens power (D) × decentration (cm); P = Fc
Base direction: base-in (BI) = convergence aid; base-out (BO) = divergence; base-up/down for vertical deviations
Fresnel prisms: thin press-on plastic prisms; lightweight; reduce image quality; used for temporary/large corrections
Minimum deviation: prism bends light most efficiently at this angle; used in spectrophotometry
Lenses
Single Curved Surface
Vergence equation: n'/v = n/u + (n'-n)/r; where r = radius of curvature
Power of surface: P = (n' - n) / r
Thin Lens
Lensmaker's equation: P = (n_lens - 1)(1/r₁ - 1/r₂)
Focal length: f = 1/P (in metres); positive f = converging; negative f = diverging
Real image: formed by converging rays on same side as outgoing light
Virtual image: rays appear to diverge from a point; cannot be projected on screen
Thick Lens / Principal Planes
Front and back principal planes (H and H'): reference planes for measuring object/image distances in thick lens system
Nodal points: ray aimed at front nodal point exits parallel from back nodal point (no angular deviation)
Back vertex power (BVP): power measured from back surface to rear focal point; what lensmeter measures
Magnification
Transverse (lateral) magnification: m = -v/u = image height / object height; negative = inverted
Axial (longitudinal) magnification: m_axial = m_transverse²; always positive (same orientation axially)
Angular magnification: ratio of angles subtended; relevant for telescopes and magnifiers
Astigmatism & Sturm
Regular astigmatism: two principal meridians at 90° to each other; correctable with cylinder lens
Conoid of Sturm: 3D optical figure formed by astigmatic pencil; two focal lines at 90°; circle of least confusion at spherical equivalent between them
Spherical equivalent (SE): SE = sphere + ½ cylinder; represents best single focal point
Power cross: shows power in each meridian of cylinder lens
WTR astigmatism: steeper meridian horizontal (90°); corrected with minus cylinder axis 180° or plus cylinder axis 90°
ATR astigmatism: steeper meridian vertical (180°); corrected with minus cylinder axis 90°
Aberrations
Chromatic aberration: different wavelengths focus at different distances; blue light focuses anteriorly; basis of duochrome/bichrome test
Spherical aberration: peripheral rays focus differently than paraxial rays; reduced by aspheric IOLs
Coma: off-axis aberration; comet-shaped blur
Wavefront theory: ideally flat wavefront; aberrations described as wavefront deviations from ideal sphere
Zernike polynomials: mathematical description of wavefront aberrations; lower order (defocus, astigmatism) and higher order (coma, trefoil, spherical)
Point spread function (PSF): image of a point source; affected by all aberrations; ideal = Airy disc
Mirrors & Telescopes
Concave mirror: f = r/2; converging; vergence equation: V' = V + 2n/r; inverts image when object beyond f
Convex mirror: diverging; virtual, erect, minified image; used as rear-view mirrors
Astronomical telescope: objective (plus) + eyepiece (plus); inverted image; M = -f_objective/f_eyepiece
Galilean telescope: objective (plus) + eyepiece (minus); erect image; M = -f_objective/f_eyepiece; shorter, field of view narrower
Spectacle magnifier: simple plus lens used close to eye; M = D/4 (for standard 25cm reference distance)
Physical Optics
Nature of Light
Visible light: 400nm (violet) → 700nm (red); electromagnetic radiation
Wave-particle duality: wavelike (interference, diffraction) and particle (photoelectric effect, photons)
Photon energy: E = hf = hc/λ; shorter wavelength = higher energy; UV > visible > IR
Polarization
Unpolarized light: electric field in all planes; polarized: restricted to one plane
Brewster's angle: angle at which reflected light is fully polarized (tangent = n)
Clinical use: polarized filters in Worth 4-dot test, Titmus stereoacuity, reducing glare (sunglasses)
Interference & Coherence
Constructive interference: waves in phase → amplitude adds; destructive: out of phase → cancellation
Coherence: temporal (same frequency) and spatial (same wavefront origin); required for interference
Anti-reflection coating: thin film of thickness λ/4 → destructive interference of reflected light
OCT: uses low-coherence interferometry (Michelson interferometer) to measure optical path length differences
Diffraction
Light bends around edges of apertures; limits optical resolution
Rayleigh criterion: minimum resolvable angle θ = 1.22λ/D; D = aperture diameter
Airy disc: central bright spot of diffraction pattern; 84% of light energy; smaller disc with larger pupil
Diffractive IOLs: use diffraction rings to split light to multiple focal distances
Measures of Light
Radiometry: physical energy (watts); irradiance = W/m²
Photometry: perceptual (visual system weighted); luminance (candela/m²); illuminance (lux = lm/m²)
Luminous efficiency function: peaks at 555nm (photopic); 507nm (scotopic)
Lasers
Fundamentals
LASER: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
Properties: monochromatic, coherent, collimated (low divergence)
Components: gain medium (determines wavelength) + pump energy + optical cavity (mirrors)
CW (continuous wave) vs pulsed; pulsed: Q-switching (nanoseconds), mode-locking (femtoseconds)
Laser-Tissue Interactions
Photocoagulation: thermal; protein denaturation; retinal laser (Argon 514nm, green; 532nm), trabeculoplasty
Photodisruption: ionisation → plasma → shock wave; Nd:YAG (1064nm); posterior capsulotomy, PI
Photoablation: UV photons break molecular bonds directly; cold; Excimer laser (ArF 193nm); LASIK, PRK
Photodynamic therapy (PDT): photosensitiser (verteporfin) + 689nm laser → singlet oxygen → vessel occlusion; wet AMD (historical/niche)
Selective laser trabeculoplasty (SLT): 532nm, Q-switched; selectively targets pigmented TM cells; no thermal damage; repeatable
Common Ophthalmic Lasers
Argon (514nm green/488nm blue-green): retinal photocoagulation, trabeculoplasty (ALT)
Nd:YAG (1064nm): capsulotomy, PI, vitreolysis
Diode (810nm): retinal photocoagulation, cyclophotocoagulation (CPC)
Excimer ArF (193nm): corneal ablation (LASIK, PRK, SMILE, PTK)
Pascal/IRIDEX pattern scan: multi-spot retinal laser; faster; less pain
Light hazards: thermal retinal damage (infrared), photochemical (UV, blue light); ANSI MPE (maximum permissible exposure) standards; protective eyewear mandatory in laser suites
Optics of the Human Eye
Schematic Eye
Gullstrand schematic eye: total power ~60D (cornea ~43D + lens ~17D); axial length 24mm
Reduced eye: simplified 1-surface model; n=4/3; P=60D; nodal point 7mm behind cornea
Important axes: optical axis (through centres of curvature); visual axis (fovea to fixation point through nodal point); pupillary axis; line of sight
Angle kappa: angle between pupillary axis and line of sight; positive kappa = fovea temporal to optical axis (common); important in LASIK centration
Pupil & Resolution
Optimal pupil: ~3mm for best resolution (balance between diffraction and aberration)
Large pupil: aberrations dominate; halos, glare; reduced by miosis (bright light, pilocarpine)
Small pupil: diffraction dominates; reduced resolution; pinhole effect (depth of focus increases)
Photopic vision: cones; 3-6mm pupil; daylight
Scotopic vision: rods; >6mm pupil; night
Visual Acuity
Minimum angle of resolution (MAR): finest detail resolved; normal = 1 arc minute (20/20 = MAR of 1')
LogMAR: logarithm of MAR; 20/20 = logMAR 0.0; 20/200 = logMAR 1.0; 20/10 = logMAR -0.1
Snellen fraction: 20/X = testing distance / distance at which letter subtends 5' arc
ETDRS chart: 5 letters per line, each line 0.1 logMAR; more accurate than Snellen; standard for clinical trials
VA influenced by: contrast, luminance, pupil size, refractive error, media clarity, retinal/cortical function
Contrast Sensitivity
CSF (contrast sensitivity function): peaks at 3-6 cpd (cycles per degree); falls off at low and high spatial frequencies
Pelli-Robson chart: letter contrast threshold at constant size; tests overall CSF
CS reduced in: early cataract, optic neuritis, glaucoma, amblyopia, before Snellen VA drops
Important for functional vision: driving, face recognition, low-light performance
Refractive States
Emmetropia: parallel rays focus exactly on retina (with relaxed accommodation)
Myopia: focus in front of retina; axial (longer eye, most common) or refractive (steeper cornea)
Hyperopia: focus behind retina; axial (shorter eye) or refractive; latent hyperopia masked by accommodation
Astigmatism: different powers in different meridians; regular (orthogonal principal meridians) or irregular
Far point: conjugate of fovea with relaxed accommodation; myope: real, in front of eye; hyperope: virtual, behind eye
Accommodation & Presbyopia
Accommodation: ciliary muscle contracts → zonules relax → lens rounds → increases power; amplitude decreases with age
Amplitude of accommodation: max (16D at age 8) → ~1D at age 50 (presbyopia)
Near point: closest point of clear focus; near point = 1/amplitude of accommodation
Presbyopia: insufficient amplitude for comfortable near work; onset ~age 40-45 years
Cycloplegia: pharmacologic paralysis of ciliary muscle → reveals full latent hyperopia; cyclopentolate 1% (children), cyclopentolate 1% or tropicamide 1% (adults)
Myopia Development & Control
Risk factors: near work, low outdoor time, Asian ethnicity, parental myopia
Emmetropisation: guided by image quality; peripheral defocus hypothesis: myopic defocus signals stop; hyperopic defocus signals growth
Control strategies: low-dose atropine 0.01% (reduces progression ~50%); orthokeratology; MiSight contact lenses; DIMS spectacle lenses; outdoor time >2hrs/day
Clinical Refraction
Retinoscopy
Principles
Observing reflex from patient's retina; when far point is at retinoscope → neutralisation (bright, full, no motion)
WTM reflex: far point behind examiner → patient is hyperopic or less myopic than working distance; ADD PLUS
ATM reflex: far point between patient and examiner → patient more myopic than working distance; ADD MINUS
Working distance correction: subtract +1.50D (67cm) or +2.00D (50cm) from total lenses in place
Retinoscopy Technique
Streak retinoscope: rotate sleeve to control convergence (spot/streak) and direction; use streak parallel to meridian being assessed
Neutralise most plus meridian first (least against motion or most with motion)
Reflex quality: narrow reflex = far from neutralisation; wide bright reflex = near neutralisation
Scissors reflex: irregular astigmatism (keratoconus); reflex splits like scissors
Pseudoneutralisation: reflex appears neutral at distance but is not true neutralisation; verify with subjective
Cycloplegic retinoscopy: mandatory in children; reveals true refractive error; subract +0.75 to +1.25D for residual cycloplegia effect
Subjective Refraction
Cross-Cylinder (JCC) Technique
Jackson Cross Cylinder (JCC): ±0.25D or ±0.50D cylinder lens; finds cylinder axis then power
Axis refinement: JCC axes at 45° to estimated cylinder axis; patient chooses preferred position → rotate axis toward minus axis of preferred position
Power refinement: JCC axes aligned with cylinder axis; patient chooses preferred position → add or subtract cylinder power
Rule: for every 0.50D cylinder added, reduce sphere by 0.25D (maintains circle of least confusion on retina)
Refining the Sphere
Fogging: start with +2 to +3D extra plus → all lines blurred; slowly reduce plus until best VA (prevents over-minusing)
Duochrome (bichrome) test: chromatic aberration principle; red light focuses behind green; balanced when green = red sharpness; more plus if green clearer; more minus if red clearer
'Maximum plus for best VA': endpoint of sphere refinement
Binocular Balance
Goal: equal accommodation in both eyes
Prism dissociation: BO prism one eye creates vertical diplopia; patient reports equal sharpness
Fogging technique: fog one eye with +1D, refine other, then swap
Cycloplegic vs non-cycloplegic: cycloplegic gives maximum plus; non-cycloplegic used for final spectacle Rx; adjust for residual accommodation
Prescription Writing
Format: Sphere / Cylinder × Axis (e.g., -2.50 / -1.25 × 180)
Transposition: to change sign of cylinder; 1) algebraically add sphere + cylinder to get new sphere; 2) change sign of cylinder; 3) change axis by 90°
Minus vs plus cylinder convention: ophthalmologists typically use minus cylinder; optometrists may use plus cylinder; same Rx different notation
Eyeglasses
Spectacle Correction Principles
Far point concept: correcting lens must place image of infinity at patient's far point
Vertex distance: distance from back of spectacle lens to cornea (~12-14mm); critical for high powers; changing vertex distance changes effective power
Vertex distance effect: for powers >±4D, must account for vertex distance in conversion from trial frame to spectacle Rx; formula: P_actual = P_trial / (1 - d × P_trial)
Pantoscopic tilt: spectacle frame tilts down anteriorly; introduces small cylinder effect; correct by adjusting axis
Prescribing by Refractive Error
Myopia: full correction improves distance VA; undercorrecting may worsen progression (controversial); overcorrecting causes asthenopia

Hyperopia: young patients may not need full correction (accommodate to compensate); prescribe full correction if: accommodative ET, amblyopia risk, symptoms; prescribe partial correction if asymptomatic
Astigmatism: full correction if VA improves and tolerated; axis accurate within 5-10° for large cylinders
Anisometropia: spectacles cause aniseikonia (image size difference) for large differences; CLs minimise this; prism imbalance at near (Prentice's rule)
Accommodation Problems
Presbyopia: onset ~45 years; amplitude <3D; add power based on working distance and residual amplitude
Add power calculation: working distance power - (reserve amplitude kept = ½ total); e.g., working at 40cm = 2.5D; reserve ½ of 1D amplitude; add = 2.5 - 0.5 = +2.0D
Accommodative insufficiency: reduced amplitude for age; add power required
Accommodative excess/spasm: pseudomyopia; cycloplegia reveals true Rx; treat with rest and cycloplegia
AC/A ratio: prism dioptres of convergence per dioptre of accommodation; normal 4:1 to 6:1; high AC/A → over-converge with near work
Multifocal Lenses
Bifocals
Flat-top (FT28/FT35): most common; D-shaped segment; sharp segment edge
Round segment: older style; round add segment
Executive (full-width): segment runs full width; used in children with accommodative ET (clear full near field)
Image jump: prismatic effect at segment top; occurs in round segments; absent in executive
Segment height: usually at lower limbus; adjusted for occupation
Trifocals: add distance, intermediate (~+1.5 add), and near adds; intermediate for arm's length work
Progressive Addition Lenses (PALs)
Continuous power change from distance (top) to near (bottom); no visible line
Corridor: narrow channel of clear vision; peripheral distortion ('swim effect') in lateral zones
Hard design: narrow corridor, less distortion in centre; soft design: wider corridor, more peripheral distortion
Advantage over bifocal: no image jump, better cosmesis, intermediate vision; disadvantage: adaptation needed, distortion

Prentice's rule in bifocals: prismatic imbalance at near due to different powers; BO prism in myopes (base splits = tolerating); BI in hyperopes; slab-off prism corrects vertical imbalance in anisometropia
Special Lenses
Aphakic spectacles: ~+12D; high plus; significant magnification (~25-30%); ring scotoma; jack-in-the-box effect; replaced by IOLs and CLs
Absorptive lenses: tinted, photochromic (darken in UV), polarized; UV protection important for all sunglasses; UV400 blocks UV-A and UV-B

Lens materials: glass (heavy, best optics, shatters), CR-39 plastic (standard), polycarbonate (impact-resistant, high n=1.586, mandatory for children/athletes), Trivex (similar to poly, better optics), high-index (n=1.67, 1.74; thinner for high powers)
Anti-reflection (AR) coating: reduces reflections; improves cosmesis and VA; multilayer thin film; increases scratch susceptibility
Prism in spectacles: base direction and Prentice's rule; slab-off for vertical anisometropic imbalance; yoked prisms (both BO or both BI) for field loss
Pharmacologic presbyopia treatment: pilocarpine 1.25% (Vuity) drops; creates miosis → increased depth of focus; trade-off dim light/night driving
Contact Lenses
Contact Lens Optics
Tear lens: fluid between contact lens and cornea acts as lens; for RGP CLs, liquid tear film fills corneal irregularities (corrects irregular astigmatism)
Anisometropia and image size: CLs closer to nodal point → less aniseikonia than spectacles; preferred for >3D anisometropia
Accommodation: myopes need less accommodation with CLs than spectacles (reverse for hyperopes)
Correcting astigmatism: toric soft CLs (cylindrical correction) or RGP CLs (tear lens masks corneal astigmatism; only lenticular astigmatism needs correction)
Contact Lens Materials
Hydrogel soft CL: HEMA-based; Dk (oxygen transmissibility); standard daily/monthly wear; water content 38-75%
Silicone hydrogel: high Dk (Dk/t >100); allows extended/continuous wear; stiffer, more deposits; replaces hydrogel as standard
RGP (rigid gas-permeable): PMMA-derived + silicone-acrylate; high Dk; durable; better optics; corrects irregular astigmatism; adaptation period needed
Scleral RGP: large diameter (>18mm); vaults cornea completely; fluid reservoir; for keratoconus, irregular cornea, severe dry eye, post-surgical
Fitting Principles
Soft CLs
Base curve (BC): slightly flatter than flat K; standard 8.4 or 8.6mm
Diameter: usually 14-14.5mm; covers limbus
Fit assessment: centration, coverage, movement (0.5-1.0mm on blink), push-up test
Too tight: no movement, conjunctival injection, 3 and 9 o'clock staining; Too loose: excessive movement, edge stand-off
RGP CLs
Three-point touch: slight apical clearance + mid-peripheral bearing + edge lift
Fluorescein pattern: green pooling = clearance; dark = bearing; assess with cobalt blue
SAM (Steeper Add Minus): steeper BC → more minus power needed; FAP (Flatter Add Plus)
Steep fit: bubble under lens; excessive corneal moulding; flat fit: edge fluting, poor centration
Special Applications
Toric soft CLs: stabilisation mechanisms: prism ballast (base-down weight), truncation, peri-ballast; stability critical; rotation documented and Rx adjusted
Multifocal CLs for presbyopia: simultaneous vision (concentric or aspheric zones); modified monovision (dominant eye distance, non-dominant near)
Orthokeratology (OrthoK): reverse geometry RGP worn overnight; reshapes cornea temporarily; corrects low-medium myopia; approved for myopia control; monitor for Acanthamoeba risk
Bandage CLs: silicone hydrogel on extended wear; protects epithelium; for RCE, bullous keratopathy, post-PKP; must add topical antibiotic
Care and Complications
Solutions: multipurpose (clean/rinse/disinfect/store); hydrogen peroxide (most effective disinfection; neutralise before insertion); saline (rinse only)
No tap water: risk of Acanthamoeba keratitis
Complications
Microbial keratitis: Pseudomonas (soft CL), Acanthamoeba (OrthoK/tap water); sight-threatening emergency
Hypoxic: corneal neovascularisation (extended wear), corneal oedema, polymegethism (endothelium), epithelial microcysts
GPC (giant papillary conjunctivitis): immune response to deposits; giant papillae superior tarsus; switch to daily disposables or take break
CLPC/superior epithelial arcuate lesion (SEAL): mechanical; superior limbal arcuate SPK; flatten or reduce wearing time
3 and 9 o'clock staining: desiccation at margins of RGP; improve tear film, increase lens diameter
Contact lens-induced acute red eye (CLARE): inflammatory; hypoxia + bacterial toxins; discontinue CL; manage with topical antibiotics ± steroids
Intraocular Lenses
IOL Power Calculation
Biometry Parameters
Axial length (AL): most critical; 1mm error → ~2.5D refractive surprise; optical biometry (IOLMaster, Lenstar) preferred over ultrasound (A-scan)
Keratometry (K readings): corneal power; measured over central 3mm; errors common post-refractive surgery
Anterior chamber depth (ACD): predicts IOL position (ELP = effective lens position)
Lens thickness, WTW, CCT: used in newer generation formulas
IOL Formulas
SRK/T: 3rd generation; regression + theoretical; still widely used; less accurate in extreme AL
Holladay 1 & 2: theoretical; Holladay 2 uses 7 parameters; better for unusual eyes
Hoffer Q: best for short eyes (<22mm); SRK/T best for long eyes (>26mm)
Barrett Universal II: 5th generation; outperforms 3rd generation in most studies; accounts for posterior cornea
Kane formula: AI-assisted; among best performers in recent studies
Hill-RBF (radial basis function): pattern recognition algorithm; excellent for normal eyes
ELP (effective lens position): predicted position of IOL principal plane; main source of formula error
IOL Calculation After Refractive Surgery
Problem 1: instrument error — keratometer/topographer overestimates corneal power post-LASIK (measures anterior only)
Problem 2: index of refraction error — keratometer assumes standard anterior/posterior ratio (altered by laser)
Problem 3: formula error — ELP predicted from K readings; if K falsely low, ELP underestimated → hyperopic surprise
Solutions: use ASCRS online calculator; Masket/Modified Masket method; Shammas no-history method; Barrett True-K (no history needed); double-K method
General rule post-myopic LASIK: expect hyperopic surprise if not corrected → aim slightly myopic target
IOL Types
Monofocal: single focal point; best distance (or near) vision; spectacle-dependent for other distances; acrylic or PMMA; most commonly implanted
Toric IOL: corrects corneal astigmatism; must align with steep corneal meridian; rotation of 1° = loss of ~3% correction; mark axis before surgery
Multifocal IOLs
Refractive multifocal: concentric zones of different powers; pupil-dependent
Diffractive multifocal: diffractive rings on posterior surface; splits light to distance + near; ReSTOR, Tecnis; halos and glare common
EDOF (extended depth of focus): elongated focal range; reduces halos vs multifocal; good intermediate; near may need glasses; Symfony, Vivity
Trifocal: distance, intermediate, near simultaneously; Panoptix; increasing popularity
Contraindications: significant ocular comorbidity (AMD, glaucoma, corneal disease), unrealistic expectations, dry eye
Accommodative IOL: flexes or moves in capsular bag to change power; Crystalens; limited accommodation in practice
Aspheric IOL: reduces spherical aberration; matches corneal prolate shape; improves contrast sensitivity, especially mesopic
Blue-light filtering IOL: blocks UV + short-wavelength visible (blue); may protect macula; slightly yellow tint; theoretical benefit controversial
Lens-Related Optical Disturbances
Halos: diffractive multifocal IOL; pupil-dependent; brain adapts (neuroadaptation) over 3-6 months
Dysphotopsia: positive (bright arc/crescent temporally) or negative (dark crescent near edge of IOL); square-edge design accentuates; may resolve spontaneously or require IOL exchange
PCO (posterior capsule opacification): Elschnig pearls; most common late complication; Nd:YAG capsulotomy; square-edge IOL reduces incidence
Glistening: tiny fluid-filled vacuoles in hydrophobic acrylic IOL; Acrysof; rarely visually significant
Special IOL Calculations
Pediatric eyes: rapidly changing AL; target hyperopia (undercorrect); avoid multifocals; aphakia preferred <1 year
Silicone oil-filled eye: change in refractive index of vitreous space; use Okulix or specific formulas; remove SO before IOL calculation if possible
Post-keratoplasty: K readings may be unreliable early; wait for suture removal and stable refraction
Optical Considerations in Keratorefractive Surgery
Corneal Shape
Normal cornea: prolate shape (steeper centrally, flatter peripherally); asphericity factor Q = -0.26 (reduces spherical aberration)
Post-myopic LASIK: cornea becomes oblate (flatter centrally); increases spherical aberration; reduces contrast sensitivity
Wavefront-guided LASIK: attempts to minimise induced higher-order aberrations; better night vision than standard treatment
Topography-guided LASIK: centres treatment on corneal apex; better for irregular corneas
Pupil & Angle Kappa
Large pupil: edge of ablation zone within optical pupil → halos and glare
Angle kappa: angle between pupillary axis and visual axis; large angle kappa → decentration of ablation if centred on pupil; centre on Purkinje reflex (coaxially sighted corneal reflex)
Optical zone: diameter of treated cornea; >6mm recommended; smaller → more aberrations and regression
Irregular Astigmatism
Definition: cannot be fully corrected with sphere + cylinder; multiple wavefront aberration terms involved
Causes: keratoconus, post-surgical (decentred ablation, corneal scar, PTK), trauma, dystrophies
Wavefront analysis (Hartmann-Shack): measures aberrations as Zernike polynomial coefficients; lower order (defocus, astigmatism) and higher order (coma, trefoil, SA)
Management: RGP/scleral CLs (tear lens masks irregular surface); wavefront-guided PRK; penetrating or lamellar keratoplasty
Munnerlyn Formula
Ablation depth (μm) ≈ D × S² / 3; D = refractive correction (D), S = optical zone (mm)
Each dioptre of myopic correction removes ~15μm for 6mm zone
Residual stromal bed: minimum 250-300μm to prevent ectasia; pachymetry pre-op essential
Keratorefractive Procedures Overview
PRK: surface ablation; removes epithelium; slower healing; haze risk (MMC prophylaxis); no flap complications; preferred for thin corneas
LASIK: microkeratome or femtosecond flap; immediate recovery; flap complications (striae, dislocation, interface deposits)
SMILE (small incision lenticule extraction): femtosecond laser creates intrastromal lenticule; extracted through small incision; no flap; less dry eye; limits enhancement
LASEK: alcohol loosens epithelium as flap; intermediate between PRK and LASIK
Optical Instruments
Refraction & Topography Instruments
Lensmeter (Focimeter/Lensometer)
Measures back vertex power (BVP) of spectacle or contact lens
Manual: telescope focused on target through unknown lens; read sphere, cylinder, and axis
Automated: automated lensmeters; also read prism (displacement of measurement target)
Multifocal: measure through distance and near zones separately
Autorefractor
Infrared beam into eye; analyses reflected image; gives quick starting estimate for refraction
Hartmann-Shack wavefront sensor: maps entire wavefront; gives both lower and higher-order aberrations
Limitations: accommodation affects result; less accurate in media opacities
Keratometer
Measures radius of curvature of central 3mm of cornea; calculates K readings (D)
Javal-Schiötz: doubling type; 2 mires; reading axis and power
Bausch & Lomb type (Placido-derived): single mire; rotating
Only measures 4 points; misses irregular astigmatism; topography needed for full corneal assessment
Conversion: r (mm) = 337.5 / K (D); approximately
Corneal Topography
Placido disc: measures anterior surface curvature from reflection of concentric rings; colour-coded map
Scheimpflug (Pentacam, Galilei): rotating camera; anterior + posterior surface + pachymetry; true elevation maps; essential for refractive surgery screening
Maps: axial (sagittal), tangential (instantaneous), elevation (above best-fit sphere); tangential most sensitive for KC
Normal: regular bowtie pattern (with-the-rule astigmatism); inferior steepening suggests keratoconus
Wavefront Aberrometers
Hartmann-Shack: array of lenslets → measures local wavefront slopes → reconstructed wavefront
Zernike decomposition: polynomial fitting; Z₂₀ = defocus, Z₂±² = astigmatism, Z₃±¹ = coma, Z₄⁰ = spherical aberration
Clinical use: guide wavefront-guided LASIK; characterise irregular astigmatism; evaluate IOL performance
Anterior & Posterior Segment Instruments
Slit-Lamp Biomicroscope
Koehler illumination: uniform illumination; slit beam adjustable width/height/angle/filter
Magnification: 6×-40× (binocular) via zoom or click-stops
Working distance: fixed; fundus contact or non-contact lenses extend to posterior segment
Goldmann Applanation Tonometry (GAT)
Measures IOP by applanating 3.06mm diameter disc; fluorescein + cobalt blue; endpoint: inner edges of two semicircles just touching
Pressure (mmHg) = force (g) × 10; reading × 10 = mmHg if force in grams
Errors: CCT (thicker cornea → overestimates; thinner → underestimates; 10μm ≈ 0.5mmHg); corneal curvature, tear film, oedema
Pascal DCT, ORA (ocular response analyser), ICare: alternatives; less affected by CCT
Ophthalmoscopy
Direct ophthalmoscope: erect virtual image; magnification ~15×; small field of view; limited by small pupil; image: erect virtual
Indirect ophthalmoscope: inverted real image; 2-5× magnification; wide field; 20D lens (3× mag, 45° field); used for peripheral retina, ROP; fundus details: INVERTED
Non-contact slit-lamp lenses: 78D (15×, 43° field), 90D (13×, 62° field), wide-field 60D; inverted image; used for posterior pole examination
Contact lenses: Goldmann 3-mirror (peripheral retina, gonioscopy); Volk Quad-aspheric; direct slit-lamp fundus view
Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT)
Principle: low-coherence interferometry; Michelson interferometer; measures optical path length; depth resolution 3-10μm
Time-domain OCT: moving reference arm; slower; 400 A-scans/sec; older generation
Spectral-domain (Fourier-domain) OCT: fixed reference; Fourier transform of spectrum; faster (25,000-100,000 A-scans/sec); better resolution; current standard
Swept-source OCT: longer wavelength (1050nm); better penetration through media opacities, visualises choroid; >100,000 A-scans/sec
OCT-A (angiography): motion contrast; detects blood flow without dye; en-face maps of capillary layers; quantifies foveal avascular zone
Clinical applications: macular diseases (AMD, DME, ERM), glaucoma (RNFL/GCC), optic nerve, anterior segment (AS-OCT)
Scanning laser ophthalmoscope (SLO): laser illumination; confocal optical section; reduces scatter; wide field imaging (Optos 200°); used with OCT and FA in modern combined platforms

Adaptive optics (AO): wavefront sensor + deformable mirror corrects ocular aberrations in real-time; enables cellular-level imaging (individual photoreceptors, ganglion cells); research tool advancing to clinical use
Specular microscopy: images corneal endothelium; cell density, polymegethism, pleomorphism; normal >2000 cells/mm²; monitor pre/post surgery
Vision Rehabilitation
Definitions
Low vision: BCVA <6/18 (20/60) to perception of light, or visual field <10° in better eye, not correctable with standard spectacles/CLs/surgery
Blindness (legal, USA): BCVA ≤6/60 (20/200) in better eye with best correction OR visual field ≤20° diameter
WHO categories: V1 (6/18-6/60), V2 (6/60-3/60), V3 (3/60-1/60), V4 (<1/60), V5 (no light perception)
Vision rehabilitation: comprehensive, multidisciplinary process; maximises remaining visual function and independence
Low Vision Evaluation
Visual acuity: ETDRS (gold standard); near VA (Jaeger, point notation, logMAR); reading speed (critical functional measure)
Refraction: careful refraction first; often missed correction in low vision patients; high adds (+4D to +12D) for near
Contrast sensitivity: Pelli-Robson; Mars Letter; predicts functional vision better than VA alone in some diseases
Central visual field: Amsler grid (10° field, qualitative); tangent screen; microperimetry (maps scotoma relative to fixation point, crucial for eccentric viewing training)
Peripheral field: Goldmann kinetic perimetry; useful for orientation + mobility planning; important in RP, glaucoma
Eccentric viewing: patient with central scotoma learns to use preferred retinal locus (PRL); assessed with scanning laser ophthalmoscope + microperimetry
Patient goals: reading, driving (eligibility), face recognition, ADLs; function-centred evaluation
Charles Bonnet syndrome: formed visual hallucinations in visually impaired (not psychotic); reassure patient; may reduce with low vision aids
Optical Devices
Magnifiers
Magnification = D/4 for simple lens at standard 25cm reference; or M = (add power) / 4 in practice
Types: stand magnifier (fixed focal distance), handheld magnifier (flexible), illuminated (improves contrast)
Trade-off: higher magnification → smaller field of view; shorter working distance (unless telemicroscope)
Spectacle Magnifiers
High-add glasses (+4D to +12D): short working distance (8-25cm); both hands free; useful for sustained reading
Telemicroscope: telescope + reading cap; longer working distance; limited field
Telescopes
Galilean (hand, spectacle-mounted): erect, wide FOV, compact; low power (2-3×)
Keplerian: higher power; erect via prism; wider FOV; heavier
Bioptic telescope: mounted high in spectacle lens; drives legally in many states with 20/40 through telescope; controversial
Miniaturised: Ocutech, IQ Galilean; spectacle-mounted for distance tasks
Prism spectacles for field loss: yoked prisms (both BO) shift image from blind field into seeing area; Peli lens for hemianopia (sector prism in periphery)
Electronic & Non-Optical Aids
CCTV / video magnifiers: electronic; variable magnification (2-60×); large screen; best for reading/writing; high contrast settings
Tablets/smartphones: built-in accessibility (zoom, VoiceOver/TalkBack, high contrast); versatile; front camera as magnifier
Screen readers: NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver; converts text to speech; for computer/tablet use
Portable electronic magnifiers: hand-held (Pebble, Merlin Go); video display; practical for daily use
AI-powered devices: OrCam MyEye, Envision Glasses; reads text, recognises faces/objects; wearable; emerging category
Non-optical: large-print materials, bold-line paper, task lighting (directed lamp), high contrast (black on white), reading stands, talking clocks
Rehabilitation for Field Loss
Hemianopia: compensatory saccade training (shift eyes into blind field); prism spectacles (Peli lens); orientation + mobility training
Constricted field (tunnel vision): reverse telescope (minifies → increases apparent field); orientation + mobility; cane/guide dog
Central scotoma (AMD): eccentric viewing training using microperimetry; steady eye strategy; low vision aids; text-to-speech
Driving: many states allow bioptic driving with 20/200 general VA and 20/40 through bioptic; visual field requirements vary
Sight Substitution & Training
Orientation & mobility (O&M) training: travel skills, cane use, guide dog training; specialist O&M trainer
Braille: tactile reading; taught by vision specialist; most effective if started young
Audio: talking books (NLS), screen readers, voice assistants (Alexa, Siri)
Retinal prostheses (Argus II, Alpha IMS): electrical stimulation of remaining retinal cells; limited resolution (light/dark discrimination) in RP; not commercially available in most markets
Cortical visual prosthesis: electrodes on visual cortex; Orion; in trials; intended for complete blindness
Pediatric Vision Rehabilitation
Early intervention: first 3 years most critical for visual and motor development; start rehabilitation as soon as visual impairment identified
VI teachers: school-based specialists; braille, ADL, orientation training; IEP development
CVI (cerebral visual impairment): most common cause in developed world; visual skills approach (light response, reach, visual field preference); NVI-CVI scale
Top 30 Exam Facts
Vergence equation: V' = V + P; power of lens in dioptres; P = 1/focal length in metres
Prentice's rule: prism power (Δ) = lens power (D) × decentration (cm); P = Fc
WTM reflex in retinoscopy → add plus; ATM → add minus; neutralisation = bright full pupil reflex, no motion
Working distance: 67cm = subtract +1.50D; 50cm = subtract +2.00D from gross retinoscopy Rx
JCC axis check: JCC axes at 45° to estimated axis; rotate toward minus axis of preferred flip
JCC power check: JCC aligned with cylinder axis; for each 0.50D cyl added, reduce sphere by 0.25D
Duochrome test: if red clearer → too much plus (overcorrected myope); if green clearer → add more plus
Snell's law: n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂; critical angle: sinθc = n₂/n₁; basis of gonioscopy contact lens
Conoid of Sturm: astigmatic beam; circle of least confusion at spherical equivalent
Transposition: new sphere = sphere + cylinder; new cylinder = same power opposite sign; new axis ± 90°
Vertex distance effect: >±4D requires adjustment when changing from trial frame to specs or specs to CL
Chromatic aberration: blue focuses anteriorly; duochrome test uses this — green targets reflect from more anterior focal point
Laser photoablation: Excimer ArF 193nm; cold; removes 0.25μm per pulse; Munnerlyn: depth ≈ D×S²/3
Nd:YAG: 1064nm; photodisruption; posterior capsulotomy, peripheral iridotomy
SLT: 532nm Q-switched; selectively targets TM cells; repeatable; no thermal scar
OCT principle: low-coherence interferometry; SD-OCT 25,000-100,000 A-scans/sec; depth resolution 3-10μm
Goldmann applanation: endpoint = inner edges of semi-circles touching; CCT affects reading (10μm ≈ 0.5mmHg)
Direct ophthalmoscopy: erect virtual image ×15; indirect ophthalmoscopy: inverted real image ×2-5; 20D lens ×3 with 45° FOV
IOL formulas: Barrett Universal II and Kane among most accurate; Hoffer Q for short eyes (<22mm); SRK/T for long eyes (>26mm)
Post-myopic LASIK IOL calc: hyperopic surprise common; use ASCRS online calculator or Barrett True-K
ELP (effective lens position): main source of IOL power error; predicted from K and ACD
RGP CL: SAM = Steeper Add Minus; FAP = Flatter Add Plus
OrthoK (orthokeratology): reverse geometry RGP worn overnight; reshapes cornea; risk of Acanthamoeba with water contact
CL-related keratitis: Pseudomonas most common; Acanthamoeba (radial keratoneuritis, PHMB treatment); never use tap water
Myopia control: low-dose atropine 0.01% ~50% reduction; OrthoK, MiSight CL, DIMS spectacles; outdoor time >2hrs/day
Low vision legal blindness (USA): BCVA ≤20/200 or visual field ≤20° in better eye with best correction
Eccentric viewing: central scotoma → preferred retinal locus (PRL); train with microperimetry; steady eye strategy
Magnification formula: M = D/4 for simple magnifier; or needed magnification = distance VA target / current VA
Slab-off prism: corrects vertical prismatic imbalance at near in anisometropia; ground on lens with more minus; base-up on that lens
PAL (progressive addition lens): no jump; peripheral distortion in lateral zones; hard vs soft design trade-off
